lines.text: Against Me!. 'Sink, Florida, Sink.' Two thousand and two. Laura Jane Grace founded this band in Naples, Florida back in '97, and by the time this record hit, they were electric, they were furious, and they were saying goodbye to the swamp.

Grace moved to Gainesville and built this thing from acoustic demos into a full-throttle punk machine. James Bowman's been there since 2001 — solid guitarist, solid anchor. The world was against her. That's what the name *means*.

This track is about drowning in your hometown. About a place that won't let you breathe. I think we all know that feeling tonight. We're {{ELAPSED}} in and some of you are feeling the walls closer than usual.

Grace didn't come out as trans until 2012, but the dysphoria was already there in songs like this — that sense of being at war with where you come from, with who everyone thinks you are. She built Transgender Dysphoria Blues later, but the hunger started here.
The whole album's like watching someone realize their hometown is quicksand. Perfect for 04:00. You're tired. You're thinking about things you shouldn't. This song knows that.

The Bouncing Souls. 'Ole.' Nineteen ninety-nine. These guys are from New Brunswick, New Jersey — Greg Attonito, Pete Steinkopf, Bryan Kienlen, all high school friends from Basking Ridge who decided to move to a college town and make some noise.

They named themselves after Doc Martens. The 'Bouncing Soles' slogan. That's the kind of punk rock stupid-smart that matters. They founded their own label, Chunksaah Records, played shows nobody wanted them to play, and built a community of 'True Believers.'

Hopeless Romantic, which had this track, dropped in '99 and it was their gateway into Epitaph. Personal lyrics. Fast. Light-hearted. They brought back the pogo, and New Jersey punks started sounding like they were having *fun* again.

Here's the thing about this song: it's a singalong. It's built for people standing together, raising their voices. Right now, at 04:00, you're probably alone in your room, barricaded, exhausted. But if you're singing this — and you should be — you're not alone.
The Souls went through drummer changes, personal problems, lineup turbulence. But they never stopped believing in the community. That's the *Bouncing Souls* move.

Hot Water Music. 'Trusty Chords.' Nineteen ninety-eight. Gainesville, Florida. Chuck Ragan and Chris Wollard sharing vocals, Jason Black on bass, George Rebelo on drums. Named after a Charles Bukowski short story collection.

These guys formed in '94 because Black convinced the others that Gainesville had a real music scene worth moving for. They were in each other's bands — Fossil, Thread — before they became Hot Water Music. Four friends who trusted each other enough to make something permanent.

The dual vocals on this — Ragan and Wollard doing call-and-response — that's their signature. It's gritty. It's raw. It's two guys shouting about resilience and working-class struggle and not giving up even when the touring schedule is destroying you.

They broke up in August '98. Exhaustion. Strain. Relationships couldn't take it. But then they rehearsed for a farewell show in Gainesville on August 28th, and something *clicked*. They wrote four new songs during those rehearsals. They realized breaking up wasn't the answer.
Ragan told the crowd, 'I don't know what to tell you guys.' They stayed together. That's the Hot Water Music story. Friendship over everything. Even when it hurts.

tracks.title: Sunday Morning
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Sunday Morning.' Nineteen ninety-seven. These guys are a supergroup cover band — Spike Slawson from Swingin' Utters, C.J. Ramone, Joey Cape, Fat Mike from NOFX, John Reis. They formed in San Francisco because they needed something to do when their main bands weren't touring.

Their gimmick: they take songs from every genre imaginable and punk them up so hard they barely sound like the original. Have a Ball covered the '60s and '70s. Are a Drag was Broadway show tunes punked into oblivion. They don't take themselves seriously, which is the most punk rock thing you can do.

Fat Mike, according to the liner notes, got so caught up in 'Wild World' that he thought he was 'Fat Stevens,' a guitar-toting Islamic extremist. When Spike sang 'All My Lovin' he mistook it for 'Allah my Lovin' and condemned Spike to death for heresy. That's the Gimmes energy.

This track — 'Sunday Morning' — is them taking something tender and making it *theirs*. Rapid-fire, ridiculous, beautiful. At 04:00 on a Purge night, Sunday morning feels very far away. But it's coming.
They've worn matching costumes at shows — cowboy outfits, drag, whatever. They played a kid's Bar Mitzvah and made mistakes and owned it. That's the spirit I need right now.

The Lawrence Arms. 'Coming Home.' Two thousand and three. Chicago. Brendan Kelly on vocals and bass, Chris McCaughan on vocals and guitar, Neil Hennessy on drums. They named themselves after an apartment complex they got evicted from in the middle of the night.

Kelly spent years in ska punk before this — Slapstick. McCaughan was in the Broadways. They came together and immediately recorded their first album before they'd even played a single show. A Guided Tour of Chicago. No EPs, no singles — straight to the full-length on Asian Man Records.

The Greatest Story Ever Told, which had this track, dropped in 2003 and it came with *footnotes*. Footnotes, folks. They were referencing history, literature, pop culture so deep they had to explain themselves in the liner notes. That's scholarly punk rock.

Brendan Kelly once said — and I'm paraphrasing, but the spirit is true — that they write about experiences shaped by alcohol, by chaos, by being young and lost in a city that doesn't care. Dark humor mixed with anthems. Beauty in the mess.
'Coming Home' is about that moment when you realize you don't have a home to come to. Or maybe you do and it won't let you in. Either way, 04:00 is when you feel that the deepest.

NOFX. 'Linoleum.' Nineteen ninety-four. Punk in Drublic. This is the album that changed everything. Fat Mike — Michael Burkett — Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin, El Hefe. Los Angeles. Fairfax High School. Four guys who decided the world was too stupid for subtlety.

Punk in Drublic hit number twelve on the Heatseekers chart and went gold six years later. Gold. A NOFX album went *gold*. But here's the thing — Fat Mike made the 'Leave It Alone' video and decided *not* to send it to MTV. He didn't want to be part of that machine.

The band's been political since day one. They've written about racism, about middle-class collapse, about drug abuse — 'Six Years on Dope' details everything substance abuse cost them. They became more leftist as the years went on. They gave zero fucks about being palatable.

'Linoleum' itself is about economic anxiety, about scraping by, about the floor beneath your feet being the cheapest material possible because that's all you can afford. It was true in 1994. It's still true in 2169.

Fat Mike's done a lot of things — started Fat Wreck Chords, signed bands, performed as Cokie the Clown at SXSW, debuted a song called 'Drinking Pee.' He's lived. He's suffered. He's made art out of it. This track is proof.
We're at 04:00 Madison time and this song gets it. The floor is linoleum. The walls are thin. You're doing your best. That's all anybody can do.
